1:
"And God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged;" The witnesses note God remembering Noah and sending a wind to assuage the waters. On "God remembered Noah, and every living thing... in the ark," the grammatical voices note the divine remembrance. Gill and Ellicott note that "God remembered Noah, and every living thing... in the ark" — God remembered Noah and the creatures with him; this is spoken "after the manner of men" (an anthropopathy), not that God had forgotten (God forgets nothing), but that God now turned in mercy to act for Noah's deliverance; God's "remembering" is his gracious turning to fulfill his covenant and to deliver the remnant.

On "God remembered Noah... that was with him in the ark," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the faithfulness. Henry and Calvin note that God remembered Noah and his covenant with him (6:18); through the long months of the flood, when it might have seemed Noah was forgotten, God remembered him; God's faithful care for his own, who are never forgotten by him; the turning point of the narrative — from judgment to deliverance.

On "God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the means: "God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged (subsided)" — God sent a wind (as he later divided the Red Sea by a wind) to dry up and assuage the waters; the waters began to subside; God's command over the elements; the means by which the flood began to abate; the beginning of the earth's renewal. The verse displays God remembering Noah and sending a wind to assuage the waters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the remembrance — "God remembered Noah, and every living thing... in the ark," God turning in mercy to deliver Noah (an anthropopathy, not that God forgot, Gill, Ellicott); God remembering his covenant, his faithful care for his own (6:18, Henry, Calvin); "God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged," the wind by which the waters subsided (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the remembered Noah. The verse marks the remembrance: of Noah. The verse displays the covenant: kept. The verse displays the wind: sent. The verse displays the waters: assuaged. The remembrance displays God turning to deliver Noah and the creatures. The verse displays the God: remembering. The verse displays the wind: passing. The verse displays the flood: abating. God remembering Noah and sending a wind to assuage the waters displays the turn from judgment to deliverance: God remembered Noah and all in the ark, and made a wind pass over the earth, and the waters subsided — God's gracious turning to deliver the remnant and to begin the earth's renewal. The verse displays the assuaging waters, with the deep and the rain stopped (v.2).
2:
"The fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained;" The brief verse notes the stopping of the deep and the windows of heaven. The grammatical voices note the cessation of the flood's sources. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained" — the two sources of the flood (the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven, 7:11) were now stopped; the subterranean waters ceased to gush, the floodgates of heaven were closed, and the rain was restrained; the flood ceased to increase, and the waters began to abate.

On "the fountains... of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped," the witnesses note the closing. Gill and Keil note that the very sources that had been opened (7:11) were now stopped; God, who had loosed the waters in judgment, now restrained them in mercy; the closing of the deep and the heavens marks the turning of the flood.

On "the rain from heaven was restrained," the grammatical-historical note the end of the rain: "the rain from heaven was restrained (held back)" — the forty days' rain (7:12) ceased; no more water came down; with the sources stopped and the wind drying (v.1), the waters began to recede; the flood, having reached its term, began to abate at God's command. The verse displays the stopping of the deep and the windows of heaven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cessation — "the fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained," the two sources of the flood stopped, the flood ceasing to increase (7:11-12, Keil, Gill); the sources that were opened now stopped, God restraining the waters in mercy (Gill, Keil); "the rain from heaven was restrained," the rain ceasing, the waters beginning to recede (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the stopped sources. The verse marks the deep: stopped. The verse displays the windows: closed. The verse displays the rain: restrained. The verse displays the flood: abating. The cessation displays the flood's sources stopped by God. The verse displays the fountains: closed. The verse displays the heavens: shut. The verse displays the waters: receding. The stopping of the deep and the windows of heaven displays the turning of the flood: the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain restrained — the sources of the flood closed at God's command, and the waters beginning to abate. The verse displays the abating waters, returning from the earth (v.3).
3:
"And the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated." The brief verse notes the waters abating after the hundred and fifty days. The grammatical voices note the steady decrease. Ellicott and Gill note that "the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated" — the waters steadily returned (receded) from the earth, going and returning (ebbing); after the hundred and fifty days (7:24, the period of prevailing), the waters were abated (decreased); the flood, having reached its height, now steadily diminished.

On "the waters returned from off the earth continually," the witnesses note the receding. Gill and Calvin note that the waters "returned... continually (going and returning)" — the waters receded gradually and steadily, not suddenly; the flood diminished by degrees; God's ordered way of abating the waters, that the earth might dry and the ark settle.

On "after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the period: "after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated" — at the close of the hundred and fifty days during which the waters prevailed (7:24), they began to abate; the appointed period of the flood's height ended; the waters decreased, and the ark would soon rest (v.4); the flood ran its appointed course before abating. The verse displays the waters abating after the hundred and fifty days.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the abating — "the waters returned from off the earth continually... the waters were abated," the waters steadily receding after the hundred and fifty days (7:24, Ellicott, Gill); "returned... continually," the gradual, ordered receding (Gill, Calvin); "after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated," the appointed period ended, the waters decreasing (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the receding waters. The verse marks the waters: returning. The verse displays the receding: continual. The verse displays the period: 150 days. The verse displays the abating: begun. The decrease displays the waters steadily receding after the appointed period. The verse displays the waters: returning. The verse displays the days: ended. The verse displays the flood: abating. The waters abating after the hundred and fifty days display the receding of the flood: the waters returned from off the earth continually, and after the hundred and fifty days were abated — the flood, having prevailed its appointed period, now steadily receding at God's command. The verse displays the abating waters, the ark resting on Ararat (v.4).
4:
"And the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat." The witnesses note the ark resting on the mountains of Ararat. On "the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month," the grammatical voices note the dated resting. Ellicott and Gill note that "the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month" — as the waters abated, the ark settled and rested; the date is given precisely; the ark, which had floated on the prevailing waters, now came to rest on the mountains as the waters receded.

On "the ark rested," the puritan Henry notes the rest after the storm. Henry notes that the ark "rested" — after the long tossing on the flood, the ark came to rest; God brought the ark and its passengers to a settled place; the rest after the judgment; the figure of the believer's rest after the storm, brought safe by God.

On "upon the mountains of Ararat," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed JFB note the location: "upon the mountains of Ararat" — the ark rested on the mountains of Ararat (a region in Armenia, the mountainous country to the north; the highest peak now called Mount Ararat); the "mountains" (plural) suggest the mountainous region rather than a single peak; the ark came to rest on the high ground first uncovered as the waters fell; the historical location of the ark's resting. The verse displays the ark resting on the mountains of Ararat.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resting — "the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day," the ark settling on the mountains as the waters abated (Ellicott, Gill); "the ark rested," the rest after the long tossing on the flood (Henry); "upon the mountains of Ararat," the ark resting on the mountainous region of Armenia (Clarke, JFB). The verse displays the rested ark. The verse marks the date: seventh month, seventeenth day. The verse displays the rest: of the ark. The verse displays the place: Ararat. The verse displays the mountains: of the region. The resting displays the ark settled on the high ground as the waters fell. The verse displays the ark: rested. The verse displays the mountains: Ararat. The verse displays the location: historical. The ark resting on the mountains of Ararat displays the settling of the ark: in the seventh month the ark rested upon the mountains of Ararat — the ark, after its long tossing on the flood, brought to rest on the mountainous region as the waters receded. The verse displays the rested ark, the mountains appearing (v.5).
5:
"And the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen." The brief verse notes the waters decreasing until the mountaintops appeared. The grammatical voices note the continued decrease. Gill and Ellicott note that "the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen" — the waters steadily decreased; by the first day of the tenth month, the tops of the mountains became visible; the gradual uncovering of the earth as the flood receded; the dated progress of the drying.

On "the waters decreased continually," the witnesses note the steady receding. Gill notes that the waters "decreased continually (going and decreasing)" — the steady, ordered diminishing of the flood; the waters fell by degrees, day by day; God's gradual way of drying the earth.

On "the tops of the mountains seen," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the appearing land: "were the tops of the mountains seen" — the highest points of the mountains became visible as the waters fell; the first sight of the emerging earth; a sign of hope to Noah that the flood was abating and the earth would be dry; the gradual reappearance of the land. The verse displays the waters decreasing until the mountaintops appeared.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the decrease — "the waters decreased continually until the tenth month... were the tops of the mountains seen," the waters steadily decreasing, the mountaintops appearing (Gill, Ellicott); "decreased continually," the gradual, ordered receding (Gill); "the tops of the mountains seen," the first sight of the emerging earth, a sign of hope (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the decreasing waters. The verse marks the decrease: continual. The verse displays the month: the tenth. The verse displays the mountaintops: seen. The verse displays the land: emerging. The decrease displays the waters falling until the mountains appeared. The verse displays the waters: decreasing. The verse displays the tops: seen. The verse displays the earth: emerging. The waters decreasing until the mountaintops appeared display the receding of the flood: the waters decreased until the tenth month, when the tops of the mountains were seen — the gradual uncovering of the earth, the first sight of the emerging land, a sign of hope that the flood was abating. The verse displays the appearing mountaintops, before Noah opens the window (v.6).
6:
"And it came to pass at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made:" The brief verse notes Noah opening the window of the ark. The grammatical voices note the testing of the waters. Ellicott and Gill note that "at the end of forty days... Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made" — forty days after the mountaintops appeared (v.5), Noah opened the window (the opening he had made, 6:16) to test the state of the waters; he sought to learn whether the earth was drying; Noah's careful, patient waiting and observing.

On "Noah opened the window of the ark," the puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note the patience. Henry and Calvin note that Noah, having waited patiently, now opened the window to investigate; his patience through the long months, waiting on God's time; he did not rush out but waited and tested; the patient faith of Noah, who waited for God's signal to leave the ark.

On "the window... which he had made," the grammatical-historical note the opening: "the window... which he had made" — the window (6:16), made for light and observation, is opened; through it Noah would send out the raven and the dove (vv.7-12) to learn the state of the earth; the means by which Noah, shut in by God (7:16), observed the abating of the waters. The verse displays Noah opening the window of the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the testing — "at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made," Noah opening the window to test the state of the waters (Ellicott, Gill); Noah's patient waiting, not rushing out but waiting on God's time (Henry, Calvin); "the window... which he had made," the opening (6:16) through which he sent the birds (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the opened window. The verse marks the days: forty. The verse displays the window: opened. The verse displays the testing: of the waters. The verse displays the patience: of Noah. The opening displays Noah testing the state of the earth from the window. The verse displays the window: opened. The verse displays the Noah: observing. The verse displays the waters: tested. Noah opening the window of the ark displays his patient testing of the waters: at the end of forty days Noah opened the window he had made — to learn whether the earth was drying, his patient faith waiting on God's time and observing the abating flood. The verse displays the opened window, from which the raven is sent (v.7).
7:
"And he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth." The brief verse notes Noah sending forth the raven. The grammatical voices note the first bird sent. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth" — Noah first sent out a raven to test the state of the earth; the raven "went forth to and fro" (going out and returning, or flying back and forth), not returning to the ark to stay; the raven, a carrion-bird, found enough (floating carcasses, or the mountaintops) to subsist without returning; it flew about until the waters dried.

On "a raven, which went forth to and fro," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the raven's nature. Clarke and Gill note that the raven, an unclean bird that feeds on carrion, "went forth to and fro" — it did not return into the ark (or returned only to rest on it), finding food and footing outside; the raven's nature (content with the carcasses and the bare ground) is contrasted with the dove (v.8), which sought clean rest and returned.

On "until the waters were dried up," the grammatical-historical note the testing: the raven flew about "until the waters were dried up from off the earth" — its not returning gave Noah some indication, but the raven (content with carrion and bare ground) was a less sure sign than the dove; Noah sent the dove (v.8) for a surer test of the earth's dryness. The verse displays Noah sending forth the raven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the raven — "he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up," Noah sending the raven, which flew back and forth without returning to stay (Keil, Gill); "a raven, which went forth to and fro," the carrion-bird finding food and footing outside, not returning (Clarke, Gill); the raven a less sure sign, prompting the sending of the dove (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sent raven. The verse marks the bird: a raven. The verse displays the flight: to and fro. The verse displays the nature: carrion-bird. The verse displays the testing: of the earth. The sending displays Noah testing the earth by the raven. The verse displays the raven: sent. The verse displays the flight: back and forth. The verse displays the waters: drying. Noah sending forth the raven displays the first test of the earth: Noah sent out a raven, which flew to and fro and did not return, finding food and footing on the carcasses and bare ground — a less sure sign, prompting the sending of the dove. The verse displays the sent raven, followed by the dove (v.8).
8:
"Also he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground;" The brief verse notes Noah sending forth the dove to test the waters. The grammatical voices note the second bird. Gill and Clarke note that "he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground" — after the raven, Noah sent out a dove, to test more surely whether the waters had abated from the ground; the dove, a clean bird that seeks clean rest, would return if it found no dry place; Noah used the dove as a surer means of learning the state of the earth.

On "a dove from him," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the dove's nature. Clarke and Gill note that the dove, unlike the raven, is a clean bird that loves clean, dry resting-places; the dove would not light on carrion or bare mud, but sought a clean perch; her behavior (returning, v.9) would give Noah a surer sign of whether the earth was dry.

On "to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground," the grammatical-historical note the testing: "to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground" — Noah sent the dove to learn whether the ground was dry enough; the dove's return or staying would indicate the state of the earth; the careful, patient testing of the waters; Noah's wisdom in using the birds to gather knowledge. The verse displays Noah sending forth the dove to test the waters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dove — "he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground," Noah sending the dove for a surer test (Gill, Clarke); "a dove from him," the clean bird seeking clean rest, unlike the raven (Clarke, Gill); "to see if the waters were abated," the dove's behavior to indicate the state of the earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sent dove. The verse marks the bird: a dove. The verse displays the nature: clean, seeking rest. The verse displays the testing: the waters. The verse displays the ground: tested. The sending displays Noah testing the earth by the dove. The verse displays the dove: sent. The verse displays the rest: sought. The verse displays the waters: tested. Noah sending forth the dove to test the waters displays the surer testing of the earth: Noah sent out a dove to see if the waters had abated from the ground — the clean bird, seeking clean rest, used as a surer sign of the earth's dryness. The verse displays the sent dove, returning for want of rest (v.9).
9:
"But the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark, for the waters were on the face of the whole earth: then he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark." The witnesses note the dove returning to the ark for want of rest. On "the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark," the grammatical voices note the dove's return. Gill notes that "the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark" — the dove, finding no dry place to rest (the waters still covering the earth), returned to the ark; the clean bird, unable to find clean rest, came back to Noah; the dove's return signaled that the earth was not yet dry.

On "for the waters were on the face of the whole earth," the witnesses note the still-covered earth. Gill notes that "the waters were on the face of the whole earth" — the waters still covered the ground (the low places, the valleys), though the mountaintops had appeared (v.5); there was as yet no dry resting-place for the dove; the earth was not yet ready.

On "he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark," the grammatical-historical note Noah's care: "he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in" — Noah gently received the returning dove, taking her back into the ark; his tender care for the bird; the dove found refuge again in the ark; (the dove, returning to the ark for rest, has been seen as a figure of the soul finding no rest but in Christ/the ark of safety). The verse displays the dove returning to the ark for want of rest.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark," the dove, finding no dry rest, returning to the ark (Gill); "the waters were on the face of the whole earth," the ground still covered, no resting-place (Gill); "he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in," Noah's tender care receiving the dove (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning dove. The verse marks the rest: none found. The verse displays the dove: returning. The verse displays the waters: still covering. The verse displays the care: Noah's. The return displays the dove finding no rest and coming back to the ark. The verse displays the dove: returned. The verse displays the rest: lacking. The verse displays the ark: refuge. The dove returning to the ark for want of rest displays the earth not yet dry: the dove found no rest and returned to the ark, for the waters still covered the earth, and Noah took her in — the clean bird, finding no clean rest, coming back to the refuge of the ark. The verse displays the returned dove, sent out again after seven days (v.10).
10:
"And he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark;" The brief verse notes Noah waiting seven days and sending the dove again. The grammatical voices note the patient interval. Gill and JFB note that "he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark" — Noah waited another seven days before sending the dove out again; the patient interval of waiting; the use of the seven-day period (perhaps marking the week, or the Sabbath rest) in Noah's testing; his patient, ordered waiting on God's time.

On "he stayed yet other seven days," the reformed JFB notes the patience. JFB and Gill note that Noah "stayed yet other seven days" — he waited patiently a full week before the next test; his patience and self-restraint, not rushing the matter but waiting in faith; the seven-day intervals mark his measured, patient observation of the abating flood.

On "again he sent forth the dove," the grammatical-historical note the renewed test: "again he sent forth the dove out of the ark" — Noah sent the dove a second time, to test again whether the waters had abated; the dove would now bring back a sign (the olive leaf, v.11); the patient, repeated testing by which Noah learned the state of the earth; the renewed sending of the surer bird. The verse displays Noah waiting seven days and sending the dove again.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the interval — "he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove," Noah waiting a week and sending the dove again (Gill, JFB); "he stayed yet other seven days," the patient interval, the seven-day period (JFB, Gill); "again he sent forth the dove," the renewed test of the waters (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the patient Noah. The verse marks the interval: seven days. The verse displays the patience: waiting. The verse displays the dove: sent again. The verse displays the testing: renewed. The waiting displays Noah patiently sending the dove a second time. The verse displays the days: seven. The verse displays the dove: sent. The verse displays the patience: shown. Noah waiting seven days and sending the dove again display his patient testing: Noah stayed another seven days and sent the dove out again — his patient, measured waiting on God's time, the renewed test of the abating waters. The verse displays the dove sent again, returning with the olive leaf (v.11).
11:
"And the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth." The witnesses note the dove returning with the olive leaf, the sign of abated waters. On "the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off," the grammatical voices note the sign. Gill notes that "the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off" — the dove returned at evening, bearing in her mouth a freshly plucked olive leaf; the olive leaf, plucked from a tree, showed that the waters had abated enough for the trees to appear; the sign that the earth was emerging.

On "an olive leaf pluckt off," the witnesses note the emblem. Gill notes that the "olive leaf pluckt off" — freshly plucked, green — showed that the olive trees (which grow on lower ground) were now uncovered and putting forth leaves; the olive, an evergreen, recovered quickly; the olive leaf became the emblem of peace and of the abating of God's wrath (the dove and olive branch as symbols of peace and reconciliation).

On "so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth," the grammatical-historical note the knowledge: "so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth" — by the olive leaf, Noah knew the waters had abated and the earth was emerging; the sign gave him assurance and hope; the dove and the olive leaf brought the glad tidings that the flood was passing and the earth would soon be dry. The verse displays the dove returning with the olive leaf.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sign — "the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off," the dove returning with a fresh olive leaf, the sign of abated waters (Gill); "an olive leaf pluckt off," the emblem of the emerging earth and of peace (Gill); "so Noah knew that the waters were abated," the assurance the sign gave Noah (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the olive leaf. The verse marks the dove: returning. The verse displays the leaf: olive, plucked. The verse displays the sign: abated waters. The verse displays the knowledge: Noah's. The sign displays the dove bringing news of the emerging earth. The verse displays the olive: leaf. The verse displays the peace: emblem. The verse displays the waters: abated. The dove returning with the olive leaf displays the sign of the abating flood: the dove came back at evening with a fresh olive leaf, and Noah knew the waters had abated — the olive leaf, emblem of peace and of the emerging earth, bringing the glad tidings that the flood was passing. The verse displays the olive leaf, before the dove's final sending (v.12).
12:
"And he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more." The brief verse notes the dove not returning, the sign of the dry earth. The grammatical voices note the final test. Gill notes that "he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more" — Noah waited another seven days and sent the dove a third time; this time the dove did not return — having found dry ground and rest, she stayed out; the dove's not returning was the final sign that the earth was dry and habitable.

On "he stayed yet other seven days," the witnesses note the patience. Gill notes that Noah again "stayed yet other seven days" — his continued patient waiting, the third seven-day interval; his measured, faithful patience in testing the earth before leaving the ark.

On "which returned not again unto him any more," the grammatical-historical note the dry earth: "which returned not again unto him any more" — the dove, finding the earth now dry and able to sustain her, did not return; her staying out was the sign that the flood had abated and the earth was habitable; the dove's behavior, through the three sendings, traced the drying of the earth — returning (no rest, v.9), returning with the leaf (waters abating, v.11), and not returning (the earth dry); the final sign of the renewed earth. The verse displays the dove not returning, the sign of the dry earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the final test — "he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again," the dove not returning the third time, the sign of the dry earth (Gill); "he stayed yet other seven days," the continued patient waiting (Gill); "which returned not again unto him any more," the dove finding dry ground and staying out, the final sign (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dry earth. The verse marks the interval: seven days. The verse displays the dove: not returning. The verse displays the sign: the dry earth. The verse displays the test: final. The not-returning displays the earth dry and habitable. The verse displays the dove: gone. The verse displays the earth: dry. The verse displays the sign: final. The dove not returning, the sign of the dry earth, displays the close of Noah's testing: Noah sent the dove a third time, and it did not return — having found the earth dry and able to sustain her, the final sign that the flood had abated and the earth was habitable. The verse displays the dry earth, uncovered before Noah's eyes (v.13).
13:
"And it came to pass in the six hundredth and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dry." The witnesses note the drying of the earth and Noah looking upon it. On "in the six hundredth and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up," the grammatical voices note the dated drying. Ellicott and Gill note that "in the six hundredth and first year... the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth" — on the first day of the new year (Noah's six hundred and first year), the waters were dried up from the surface; the precise dating marks the progress of the drying; the earth's surface was now dry, though not yet fully ready (v.14).

On "Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked," the puritan Henry notes the looking. Henry and Gill note that "Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked" — Noah took off the covering (the roof or a part of it) to look out upon the earth; he beheld with his own eyes the state of the ground; his careful, patient observation before leaving; he looked, and saw.

On "the face of the ground was dry," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the dry ground: "behold, the face of the ground was dry" — the surface of the earth was now dry; the waters had drained from the face of the ground; the welcome sight of the dry earth, the renewed world emerging; yet Noah still waited (v.14) for God's command before going forth. The verse displays the drying of the earth and Noah looking upon it.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the drying — "in the six hundredth and first year... the waters were dried up from off the earth," the waters dried from the surface on the new year's first day (Ellicott, Gill); "Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked," Noah's careful looking out upon the earth (Henry, Gill); "the face of the ground was dry," the surface dry, the renewed world emerging (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dry ground. The verse marks the date: new year, first day. The verse displays the waters: dried up. The verse displays the looking: Noah's. The verse displays the ground: dry. The drying displays the earth's surface dry and Noah beholding it. The verse displays the waters: gone. The verse displays the Noah: looking. The verse displays the ground: dry. The drying of the earth and Noah looking upon it display the emerging of the renewed world: on the first day of the year the waters were dried from the earth, and Noah removed the covering and saw the ground was dry — the surface of the earth dry, the renewed world emerging, though Noah still waited for God's command. The verse displays the dry ground, fully dried in the second month (v.14).
14:
"And in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried." The brief verse notes the full drying of the earth. The grammatical voices note the complete drying. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried" — by the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was fully dried (not only the surface, v.13, but the ground throughout); the earth was now firm and dry, fit for habitation; the complete drying of the earth, a little over a year after the flood began (7:11).

On "the earth dried," the witnesses note the firm ground. Clarke and Gill note that "the earth dried (was thoroughly dry)" — the ground was now solid and dry, no longer mud or marsh; the earth was fully ready to be inhabited; the distinction from v.13 (the surface dry) marks the full drying of the ground.

On the timing, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the year: from the seventeenth day of the second month (7:11, the flood beginning) to the twenty-seventh day of the second month (the earth dried) was a full year and ten days; the flood and its abating spanned about a year; God's appointed time for the judgment and the renewal; the earth, fully dried, awaited Noah's going forth at God's command (vv.15-16). The verse displays the full drying of the earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the drying — "in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried," the earth fully dried, fit for habitation (Keil, Gill); "the earth dried," the ground firm and dry, no longer mud (Clarke, Gill); the full year from the flood's beginning to the drying (7:11, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dried earth. The verse marks the date: second month, 27th day. The verse displays the earth: fully dried. The verse displays the ground: firm. The verse displays the year: complete. The drying displays the earth fully dry and fit for habitation. The verse displays the earth: dried. The verse displays the ground: firm. The verse displays the flood: ended. The full drying of the earth displays the renewal of the world: by the twenty-seventh day of the second month the earth was fully dried — firm and fit for habitation, the flood and its abating spanning about a year, the earth now awaiting Noah's going forth at God's command. The verse displays the dried earth, into which God bids Noah go (vv.15-16).
15:
"And God spake unto Noah, saying," The very brief verse notes God speaking to Noah. The grammatical voices note the divine command. Gill and Henry note that "God spake unto Noah, saying" — God now spoke to Noah, to command him to go forth from the ark (v.16); Noah, who had waited patiently (not leaving the ark until God bade him), now received the word of God; the introduction to God's command for Noah to leave the ark and the renewal of the earth.

On "God spake unto Noah," the puritan Henry notes the waiting on God's word. Henry notes that Noah waited for God to speak — though the earth was dry (v.14), Noah did not go forth until God commanded; he entered the ark at God's word (7:1) and would leave it at God's word; the patient obedience of faith, waiting on God's direction in all things.

On "saying," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the command: "saying" — God's word follows (v.16), bidding Noah go forth; God, who had shut Noah in (7:16), now opens the way for him to go out; the divine command governs Noah's entering and leaving; the believer acts on God's word; Noah's patient waiting for God's signal is the pattern of obedience. The verse displays God speaking to Noah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "God spake unto Noah, saying," God speaking to command Noah to go forth (v.16, Gill, Henry); Noah waiting on God's word, not leaving until commanded (Henry); "saying," God's word governing Noah's going out, as it had his entering (7:1, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking God. The verse marks the speaking: God to Noah. The verse displays the command: coming. The verse displays the waiting: Noah's. The verse displays the obedience: of faith. The word displays God commanding Noah to leave the ark. The verse displays the God: speaking. The verse displays the Noah: addressed. The verse displays the command: given. God speaking to Noah displays the divine direction of Noah's going forth: God spoke to Noah — to command him to leave the ark; Noah, having waited patiently for God's word, now received the divine direction, the pattern of obedience that waits on God in all things. The verse displays the speaking God, bidding Noah go forth (v.16).
16:
"Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives with thee." The very brief verse notes God's command to Noah and his household to go forth. The grammatical voices note the command to leave. Gill notes that "Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives with thee" — God commanded Noah and his whole household to go forth from the ark; the eight souls who entered (7:13) are now bidden to leave; God, who had brought them in and shut them in (7:16), now bids them go out into the renewed earth.

On "Go forth of the ark," the witnesses note the divine bidding. Gill notes that Noah was commanded to "go forth" — to leave the ark and enter the renewed world; he waited for this command (not going out of his own accord); the believer leaves the place of refuge at God's word; the orderly, obedient going forth at God's command.

On "thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives," the grammatical-historical note the household: "thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives" — the whole family (the eight souls) is to go forth together; the household preserved through the flood now goes out to repeople the earth; the family of the righteous, saved together, together enters the new world; through them the race would be renewed. The verse displays God's command to Noah and his household to go forth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives," God commanding the household to leave the ark (Gill); "Go forth of the ark," the divine bidding to enter the renewed world (Gill); "thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives," the household going forth together to repeople the earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanded going-forth. The verse marks the command: go forth. The verse displays the household: eight souls. The verse displays the ark: left. The verse displays the world: renewed. The command displays God bidding the household leave the ark. The verse displays the Noah: bidden. The verse displays the family: with him. The verse displays the going: forth. God's command to Noah and his household to go forth displays the bidding to enter the renewed world: God commanded Noah, his wife, sons, and sons' wives to go forth from the ark — the household, preserved through the flood, now bidden to leave and repeople the earth. The verse displays the commanded household, with the creatures (v.17).
17:
"Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth." The witnesses note the command to bring forth the creatures to repeople the earth. On "Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh," the grammatical voices note the creatures. Gill and Calvin note that "Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing" — God commands Noah to bring out all the creatures (fowls, cattle, creeping things) preserved in the ark; the animals, like the family, are to go forth into the renewed earth; the preserved kinds released to repopulate the world.

On "every living thing... of all flesh," the witnesses note the preserved kinds. Gill notes that all the kinds of creatures preserved in the ark are to be brought forth; the animal creation, saved through the flood, now goes out to refill the earth; the preserved pairs released to renew their kinds.

On "that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the renewed blessing: "that they may breed abundantly... and be fruitful, and multiply" — God renews the blessing of fruitfulness (1:22) upon the creatures; they are to breed, be fruitful, and multiply, refilling the earth; the renewal of the creation's fruitfulness after the flood; God's purpose to restock and replenish the world through the preserved kinds. The verse displays the command to bring forth the creatures to repeople the earth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the creatures — "Bring forth with thee every living thing... of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing," God commanding the creatures brought out to the renewed earth (Gill, Calvin); the preserved kinds released to repopulate (Gill); "that they may breed abundantly... and be fruitful, and multiply," the renewed blessing of fruitfulness (1:22, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the released creatures. The verse marks the creatures: brought forth. The verse displays the kinds: all flesh. The verse displays the blessing: fruitful and multiply. The verse displays the earth: repeopled. The command displays the creatures released to refill the earth. The verse displays the animals: brought forth. The verse displays the fruitfulness: renewed. The verse displays the earth: restocked. The command to bring forth the creatures to repeople the earth displays the renewal of the animal creation: God bade Noah bring out all the creatures, that they might breed, be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth — the preserved kinds released to refill the renewed world, the blessing of fruitfulness renewed. The verse displays the released creatures, going forth with Noah (vv.18-19).
18:
"And Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him:" The very brief verse notes Noah and his household going forth from the ark. The grammatical voices note the obedient going-forth. Gill notes that "Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him" — Noah and his whole household went forth from the ark, as God had commanded (v.16); the eight souls, preserved through the flood, now left the ark and entered the renewed earth; Noah's obedience to God's command, going forth at the divine word.

On "Noah went forth," the puritan note the obedience. Noah went forth in obedience to God's command (v.16) — not before, but when God bade him; the patient obedience of faith, waiting for God's word and then acting on it; Noah entered the ark and left it at God's direction.

On "and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him," the grammatical-historical note the household: "and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives" — the whole family went forth together; the eight souls who entered (7:13) all came out; the household of the righteous, preserved together, together entered the new world; through them the race would be renewed; the family that rode out the flood now set foot on the cleansed earth. The verse displays Noah and his household going forth from the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the going-forth — "Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him," the household going forth as God commanded (v.16, Gill); Noah's obedience, going forth at God's word (puritan); "and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives," the eight souls leaving the ark together (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the going-forth household. The verse marks the going: forth. The verse displays the household: eight souls. The verse displays the obedience: to God's command. The verse displays the earth: renewed. The going displays Noah's household leaving the ark for the new world. The verse displays the Noah: going forth. The verse displays the family: with him. The verse displays the ark: left. Noah and his household going forth from the ark display the obedient entrance into the renewed world: Noah, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives went forth — the eight souls, preserved through the flood, leaving the ark at God's command and setting foot on the cleansed earth. The verse displays the household gone forth, with the creatures (v.19).
19:
"Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark." The very brief verse notes the creatures going forth from the ark after their kinds. The grammatical voices note the released creatures. Gill notes that "Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark" — all the creatures preserved in the ark (beasts, creeping things, fowls), each after its kind, went forth into the renewed earth; the animal kinds, saved through the flood, now released to repopulate the world.

On "every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl," the witnesses note the kinds. Gill notes the classes of creatures going forth — beasts, creeping things, fowls; the comprehensive release of all the preserved kinds; the animal creation, saved in the ark, now set free upon the earth.

On "after their kinds, went forth out of the ark," the grammatical-historical note the ordered going-forth: "after their kinds, went forth out of the ark" — the creatures went forth by their kinds, in order; the distinct kinds (the species of ch. 1) preserved through the flood and now released; the renewal of the animal creation, the kinds going out to breed and multiply (v.17); the orderly repopulation of the earth by the preserved kinds. The verse displays the creatures going forth from the ark after their kinds.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the released creatures — "Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl... after their kinds, went forth out of the ark," all the preserved kinds going forth (Gill); "every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl," the classes of creatures released (Gill); "after their kinds, went forth out of the ark," the distinct kinds going out in order to repopulate the earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the released kinds. The verse marks the creatures: every beast, fowl, creeper. The verse displays the kinds: distinct. The verse displays the going: forth. The verse displays the earth: repeopled. The going displays the preserved kinds released to refill the earth. The verse displays the creatures: going forth. The verse displays the kinds: ordered. The verse displays the ark: emptied. The creatures going forth from the ark after their kinds display the release of the preserved kinds: every beast, creeping thing, and fowl, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark — the animal creation, saved through the flood, released to repopulate and renew the earth. The verse displays the released creatures, as Noah builds his altar (v.20).
20:
"And Noah builded an altar unto the LORD; and took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl, and offered burnt offerings on the altar." The witnesses note Noah building an altar and offering burnt offerings. On "Noah builded an altar unto the LORD," the grammatical voices note the first altar. Ellicott and Gill note that "Noah builded an altar unto the LORD" — Noah's first act on leaving the ark was to build an altar to the LORD; the first altar named in Scripture; Noah, delivered from the flood, devoted his first care to the worship of God; the altar was for God, an act of thanksgiving and devotion.

On "took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the clean offerings. Calvin and Gill note that Noah "took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl" — he offered the clean animals (the distinction of clean and unclean, 7:2, here serving its purpose: the extra clean beasts preserved for sacrifice); the clean creatures, fit for offering to God, were used; Noah's sacrifice was according to God's appointment.

On "offered burnt offerings on the altar," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and puritan Henry note the burnt offering: "offered burnt offerings on the altar" — Noah offered burnt offerings (whole offerings, wholly consumed, signifying entire devotion and atonement); the burnt offerings expressed his thankfulness for deliverance and his devotion to God; the sacrifice, typical of the sacrifice of Christ, was offered in faith; Noah's worship of the delivering God, the first recorded sacrifice after the flood. The verse displays Noah building an altar and offering burnt offerings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the altar — "Noah builded an altar unto the LORD," Noah's first act, building the first altar to worship God (Ellicott, Gill); "took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl," the clean animals offered (the clean preserved for sacrifice, 7:2, Calvin, Gill); "offered burnt offerings on the altar," the burnt offerings of thanksgiving and devotion, typical of Christ (Clarke, Henry). The verse displays the worshiping Noah. The verse marks the altar: built. The verse displays the offerings: clean beasts. The verse displays the burnt offerings: offered. The verse displays the worship: thanksgiving. The altar displays Noah worshiping the delivering God. The verse displays the altar: built. The verse displays the offering: burnt. The verse displays the LORD: worshiped. Noah building an altar and offering burnt offerings display his worship after deliverance: Noah's first act on leaving the ark was to build an altar and offer burnt offerings of the clean beasts — the worship of the delivering God, in thanksgiving and devotion, the sacrifice typical of Christ, offered in faith. The verse displays Noah's offering, accepted as a sweet savour (v.21).
21:
"And the LORD smelled a sweet savour; and the LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake; for the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done." The witnesses note God's acceptance of the offering and resolve never again to curse the ground — the crux of "man's heart evil from his youth." On "the LORD smelled a sweet savour," the grammatical voices note the accepted offering. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD smelled a sweet savour (a savour of rest)" — God accepted Noah's offering with favor; the anthropomorphism expresses God's gracious acceptance of the sacrifice offered in faith; the offering was pleasing to God (Ephesians 5:2, the figure of the sweet-smelling sacrifice of Christ); God was propitiated.

On "I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the resolve: God resolved not to add a further curse upon the ground, nor to repeat such a judgment (the flood); not that the curse of 3:17 is removed, but that no new curse or universal flood-judgment would come; God's gracious forbearance toward the earth and mankind.

On "for the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and the reformed Calvin note the difficulty and the meaning: this is the same fact stated in 6:5 (man's depravity), which there was the ground of the judgment; here it is given (paradoxically) as a reason for mercy and forbearance; the phrase teaches the innate, original corruption of man (evil "from his youth," from his very beginning), the doctrine of original sin (Calvin); and God, considering man's incurable weakness and depravity, in pity resolves not to repeat the curse — the very corruption that justified the flood becomes, in God's mercy, an occasion of his forbearance. On "neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done," the witnesses note the promise: God will not again smite all living things with such a flood; the universal judgment by water would not be repeated, sealed by the covenant of the rainbow (9:11-15).

The witnesses agree the substance: God accepted Noah's offering and resolved not to curse the ground again or smite all living as he had done (Keil, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin). The phrase "the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth" bears a crux: it states the innate, original corruption of man (original sin — Calvin), and functions here (paradoxically) as a reason for God's mercy and forbearance — the same fact that grounded the judgment in 6:5 now grounding God's pity on incurably weak man (Ellicott, Calvin), rather than only restating man's guilt. God's acceptance of the offering and resolve never again to curse the ground display his forbearance and a crux: the LORD smelled the sweet savour and resolved not to curse the ground or smite all living again, for man's heart is evil from his youth — the innate corruption of man, the same fact that grounded the flood (6:5) now serving, in God's mercy, as the ground of his pitying forbearance. The verse displays the resolved mercy, sealed by the promise of the seasons (v.22).
22:
"While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease." The witnesses note God's promise of the unbroken order of the seasons. On "While the earth remaineth," the grammatical voices note the duration. Ellicott and Gill note that "while the earth remaineth (all the days of the earth)" — for as long as the earth endures (until the end of the world), the promised order of nature would continue; the promise covers the whole duration of the present earth; the stability of the natural order until the consummation.

On "seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the ordered seasons. Calvin and Gill note the paired opposites: "seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night" — the regular succession of the seasons and the day and night; the ordered round of nature (sowing and reaping, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night) would continue without interruption; the faithful regularity of the natural order, by which human life is sustained.

On "shall not cease," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the promise: "shall not cease" — these would never cease (while the earth remains); God promises the unbroken continuance of the seasons and the day and night; no more would a flood interrupt the order of nature; the stability of the world is the gift of God's faithfulness; the regular order of nature, on which agriculture and life depend, is secured by God's promise; the covenant faithfulness of God in upholding the creation. The verse displays God's promise of the unbroken order of the seasons.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease," the unbroken order of the seasons promised for the whole duration of the earth (Ellicott, Gill); the regular succession of seasons and day and night, sustaining human life (Calvin, Gill); "shall not cease," the secured stability of nature, God's covenant faithfulness (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ordered seasons. The verse marks the duration: while the earth remains. The verse displays the seasons: seedtime and harvest. The verse displays the round: cold and heat, summer and winter. The verse displays the promise: shall not cease. The promise displays the unbroken order of nature secured by God. The verse displays the seasons: ordered. The verse displays the order: unbroken. The verse displays the faithfulness: God's. God's promise of the unbroken order of the seasons displays his covenant faithfulness to the creation: while the earth remains, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease — the ordered round of nature secured by God's promise, the stability of the world the gift of his faithfulness. The verse closes the account of the flood and its abating, before the covenant with Noah (ch.9).
